content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662            322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6444


October 16, 1981


Richard Staley
City Attorney
401 W. Hillcrest Drive
Thousand Oaks, CA 91360

Dear Mr. Staley:

     This is to provide an answer to the question you posed during
our telephone conversation regarding whether the City of Thousand Oaks
has any lobbying disclosure obligations under the Political Reform Act.

     The facts as I understand them are that the mayor and city
manager of Thousands Oaks have been instructed by the city council to
travel to Sacramento to attend hearings and communicate with
legislators concerning proposed legislation which may affect the city.
In addition, the city manager has been instructed to contact other city
employees to solicit their assistance in the city's lobbying efforts.
You asked whether the salary and/or reimbursements paid to the mayor
and city manager are reportable payments to influence legislative
action, or whether these payments are exempted from reporting pursuant
to Government Code Section 86300(a).

     Section 86300(a) provides that the lobbying disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act are not applicable to "any
elected public official acting in his official capacity;...."
Therefore, the salary and reimbursements paid to the mayor, as an
elected public official, are clearly exempted by this provision.

     The Commission has determined that the exclusion contained in
Section 86300(a) applies only to elected public officials and no aides,
assistants or employees acting at the direction of elected pub-
lic officials. Therefore, the city is required to report the salary
and reimbursements paid to the city manager if the city manager spends
at lease 10 percent of his or her compensated time performing lobbying
activities, and the city has incurred expenses of $250. or more during
a calendar quarter in connection with lobbying activities. (See
California Administrative Code Section 13620(d), copy enclosed.)

Richard Staley
October 16, 1981
Page 2


In addition, the city may become an employer of a lobbyist if the city
manager or any other employee meets the definition of a "lobbyist" as
set out in Government Code Section 82039. (See page 1 of the Lobbying
Information Manual, enclosed.)

     If you have any questions, please call me at the above tele-
phone number for the Technical Assistance Division.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Jeanne Pritchard
                                        Political Reform Consultant

Enclosure

sections.question: You asked whether the salary and/or reimbursements paid to the mayor and city manager are reportable payments to influence legislative action, or whether these payments are exempted from reporting pursuant to Government Code Section 86300(a).
sections.conclusion: Therefore, the city is required to report the salary and reimbursements paid to the city manager if the city manager spends at least 10 percent of his or her compensated time performing lobbying activities, and the city has incurred expenses of $2,500 or more during a calendar quarter in connection with lobbying activities. In addition, the city may become an employer of a lobbyist if the city manager or any other employee meets the definition of a 'lobbyist' as set out in Government Code Section 82039.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the City of Thousand Oaks must report salary and reimbursements paid to the mayor and city manager as lobbying disclosure obligations when they engage in legislative advocacy activities.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The mayor's salary is exempt from reporting under Government Code Section 86300(a) as an elected public official, but the city manager's salary and reimbursements must be reported as lobbying disclosure if the city manager spends at least 10% of compensated time on lobbying and quarterly expenses exceed $2,500.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document dated October 16, 1981 with some OCR errors in transcription. Question and conclusion sections identified and extracted from body text without explicit headers. City name appears inconsistently as 'Thousands Oaks' and 'Thousand Oaks' in original document. Response from Jeanne Pritchard, Political Reform Consultant.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You asked whether the salary and/or reimbursements paid to the mayor and city manager are reportable payments to influence legislative action, or whether these payments are exempted from reporting pursuant to Government Code Section 86300(a).

CONCLUSION: Therefore, the city is required to report the salary and reimbursements paid to the city manager if the city manager spends at least 10 percent of his or her compensated time performing lobbying activities, and the city has incurred expenses of $2,500 or more during a calendar quarter in connection with lobbying activities. In addition, the city may become an employer of a lobbyist if the city manager or any other employee meets the definition of a 'lobbyist' as set out in Government Code Section 82039.

embedding.summary: FPPC response regarding lobbying disclosure obligations for the City of Thousand Oaks regarding mayoral and city manager activities related to legislative advocacy. The advice clarifies that elected officials like the mayor are exempt from lobbying disclosure requirements, but employees like the city manager must be reported if lobbying thresholds are met.
